title: Define and call a function
instruction:
Use `fn` to define a function. Call it with its name and `()`.

Run this.
successMessage: Functions package behaviour you can reuse. Calling say_hello() twice runs its body twice — without repeating the code.

title: Parameters
instruction:
Parameters use `name: type` syntax. Rust always requires type annotations on function parameters.

Run this.
successMessage: Rust requires explicit types for parameters — no inference here. This makes function signatures self-documenting.

title: Return values
instruction:
Specify the return type with `->`. The last expression (without `;`) is returned.

Run this.
successMessage: x * x has no semicolon — it is an expression whose value is returned. Add a semicolon and it becomes a statement returning unit.

title: Explicit return
instruction:
Use `return` for early exits from a function.

The last expression still handles the normal case.

Run this.
successMessage: return exits immediately when n is negative. The final n at the bottom handles the positive/zero case without return.

title: Block expressions
instruction:
A block `{}` is an expression. Its value is the last expression inside it.

Run this.
successMessage: The block evaluates to 10 (3*3+1=10). This is why Rust functions implicitly return their last expression.

title: Multiple return values via tuple
instruction:
Rust functions return one value, but that value can be a tuple.

Run this.
successMessage: Tuples bundle multiple values. The caller destructures them with let (lo, hi) = .... Common pattern for functions that compute two related results.

title: Functions as arguments
instruction:
Functions can take other functions as parameters using `fn` types.

Run this.
successMessage: fn(i32)->i32 is a function pointer type. apply accepts any function with that signature. This is the foundation of higher-order programming.

title: Recursive functions
instruction:
Functions can call themselves. Every recursive function needs a base case to stop.

Run this.
successMessage: Factorial is the classic recursion example. Each call reduces n by 1 until the base case (n<=1). Rust optimises tail recursion in release mode.
